topic: Cursor Background Agents Guide 2026: Run Autonomous Coding Tasks in the Background

competitors.title: Cursor Background Agents: How They Work and Why They Matter (2026)
key_points: Multi-layered architecture: contextual awareness, task planning, autonomous execution
Key features: autonomous task handling, deep codebase awareness, conflict avoidance, feedback loops
Use cases: code refactoring, test writing, debugging, CI/CD integration
Comparison with traditional AI assistants: async vs sync, parallel vs sequential
Future outlook: longer autonomous runs, better context, multi-agent coordination

competitors.title: Cursor Background Agents: Complete Guide (2026) | Morph
key_points: Agent Mode vs Background Agents: local/in-editor vs remote cloud VM, interactive vs async, direct edits vs PR-based output
35% of Cursor's internal merged PRs are created by background agents
Cloud Agents with Computer Use (Feb 2026): each agent gets its own VM with browser, video recording, screenshots
Pricing tiers: Hobby (no agents), Pro $20/mo (overages likely), Pro+ $60/mo, Ultra $200/mo (best value), Teams $40/user/mo
MAX mode 20% surcharge on credit costs; daily agent users typically spend $60-$100/month total
Comparison with Claude Code: remote VM vs local, async PR vs interactive conversation, 70K-120K usable context vs 200K tokens
Comparison with OpenAI Codex: internet access enabled vs disabled, multiple models vs codex-1 only, browser testing vs code-only
Limitations: premature completion, cost surprises, privacy mode must be disabled, no programmatic API, security surface area
Decision framework: background agents for parallelizable well-defined tasks, interactive tools for exploratory/context-heavy work
Custom Dockerfile support for environment customization, multi-platform availability (desktop, web, mobile, Slack, GitHub)

competitors.title: Best practices for coding with agents - Cursor Blog
key_points: Agent harness has 3 components: Instructions (system prompt), Tools (file editing, search, terminal), Model (choice matters)
Start with plans: Shift+Tab for Plan Mode, agent researches codebase and asks clarifying questions before coding
Save plans to .cursor/plans/ for team documentation and resuming interrupted work
Managing context: let agent find context via grep/semantic search, use @Branch and @Past Chats
Start new conversation when: moving to different task, agent confused, finished logical unit of work
Rules (.cursor/rules/): static context for every conversation, keep focused on essentials
Skills (SKILL.md): dynamic capabilities loaded on demand, custom commands with /, hooks for before/after actions
Long-running agent loop pattern: hooks that iterate until all tests pass (grind.ts example with MAX_ITERATIONS=5)
Test-driven development workflow: write tests first, confirm they fail, then implement until they pass
Native worktree support: automatic git worktrees for parallel agents, each in isolated files/changes
Run multiple models at once for hard problems, compare results side by side
Cloud agents: delegate bug fixes, refactors, test generation, docs to remote sandboxes; trigger from cursor.com/agents, editor, or phone
Debug Mode: generates hypotheses, instruments code with logging, analyzes actual runtime data for tricky bugs
Key traits of effective agent users: specific prompts, iterate on setup, review carefully, provide verifiable goals

competitors.title: Cursor AI Statistics 2026: Users, Revenue and Adoption
key_points: Cursor annualized revenue exceeded $2.0B by February 2026, doubled from $1B in November 2025
Raised $2.3B at $29.3B post-money valuation (Series D, Nov 2025)
1M+ daily active users, 50K businesses using the product (Bloomberg Mar 2026)
Used by over half of the Fortune 500 (company disclosure Jun 2025)
JetBrains AI Pulse Jan 2026: 69% developer awareness, 18% workplace usage
Enterprise revenue share grew from ~25% (late 2024) to ~45-60% (early 2026)
Developer AI adoption: 84% using/planning AI, 51% daily AI usage (Stack Overflow 2025)

angles: Async-first development: how background agents change the developer workflow from real-time to fire-and-forget
Cost optimization pitfalls: the 20% MAX surcharge, credit consumption, and real costs of background agent usage at scale
Cloud Agents with Computer Use: visual testing, video proof, and remote desktop access for autonomous verification
Background agents vs Claude Code vs Codex: architectural differences in execution model, context, and use cases
Privacy trade-offs: disabling Privacy Mode for cloud agents, security surface area for regulated industries

Building custom environments with Dockerfiles for reproducible background agent sessions
